Question: A woman in her late 50s presented to the clinic with severe right eye pain and a headache for the past 2 days. Her ophthalmic history was significant for mixed-mechanism glaucoma due to uveitis and chronic angle closure with prior implantation of bilateral superotemporal Baerveldt drainage implants. Her intraocular pressure (IOP) was controlled with topical brinzolamide. In addition, she had Fuchs corneal dystrophy. A month earlier, she had undergone Baerveldt implant repositioning from the anterior chamber to the sulcus in the right eye in preparation for future Descemet stripping endothelial keratoplasty owing to severe corneal decompensation. At that time, visual acuity was counting fingers at 1 ft OD. She was discharged with therapy including topical steroids, antibiotics, and atropine sulfate. On the first postoperative visit, IOP was 14 mm Hg OD by Goldmann applanation. On postoperative day 11, IOP remained stable at 7 mm Hg OD by Goldmann applanation and topical atropine was discontinued. At presentation 3 weeks postoperatively, IOP was 30 mm Hg OD by Goldmann applanation. Her visual acuity worsened from counting fingers to hand motions. Slitlamp examination of the right eye revealed a superotemporal Baerveldt drainage implant with fluid over the plate. High-resolution ultrasonography was performed (Figure).High-resolution B-scan ultrasonographic image of the right eye. White arrowheads indicate the anterior and posterior borders of the tube; magenta arrowhead, the lumen of the tube; AC, anterior chamber; and C, cornea.Apply digital pressure over the plate to dislodge the obstruction What Would You Do Next?
Options: A:Apply digital pressure over the plate to dislodge the obstruction B:Reposition the tube C:Perform laser iridotomy or iridoplasty D:Perform vitrectomy
 Which option is the correct answer to the question? ALWAYS provide the option index of the final answer AT THE END with TRIPLE BACKTICKS among A, B, C, D, EXACTLY like this: The answer is ```X```. DO NOT add any other text or variations.
opa: Apply digital pressure over the plate to dislodge the obstruction
diagnosis: Acute ocular hypertension secondary to iris plugging of the Baerveldt implant
explanation: Glaucoma drainage implants (GDIs) are a mainstay of treatment for patients with glaucoma refractory to topical IOP-lowering agents.1 Several studies have demonstrated that aqueous shunts provide comparable benefits with trabeculectomy and may in fact be associated with fewer postoperative complications. In the Tube Versus Trabeculectomy Study, early postoperative complications were less frequent in the tube group compared with the trabeculectomy group, with postoperative complication rates of 21% and 37%, respectively.2Glaucoma drainage implants can be classified as either functionally valved or nonvalved. While valved tubes such as the Ahmed and Krupin GDIs have some form of flow restriction, nonvalved GDIs such as the Baerveldt and Molteno GDIs are open.3 Common postoperative complications of GDIs include a shallow or flat anterior chamber and/or hypotony, corneal edema or decompensation, choroidal effusion, hyphema, and tube obstruction.3Tube shunts may be obstructed with blood, fibrin, vitreous, or iris. In the Ahmed Baerveldt Comparison Study, the overall postoperative tube occlusion rate was 5%, and tube occlusion occurred more frequently in the Baerveldt GDI group compared with the Ahmed GDI group.4 Failure of a conjunctival bleb to form over the plate or an elevation of IOP warrants inspection of the tube for possible obstruction.3 To avoid obstruction, tubes placed in the anterior chamber are typically beveled anteriorly, whereas tubes placed in the sulcus are beveled posteriorly. In this case, the bevel was anterior as the initial tube placement was in the anterior chamber. In future cases, it would be advantageous to rebevel the tube in the event that it is moved from the anterior to posterior chamber if the length allows.Ultrasonography can be used to evaluate the patency of the tube. Management depends on the mechanism of obstruction. Occasionally, debris obstructing the tube opening can be removed with digital pressure over the plate. With blood or fibrin tube obstruction, injection of tissue plasminogen activator can dissolve the clot. If the tube is occluded by iris or inflammatory tissue, an argon or YAG laser can be used to remove the tissue. For tubes occluded with vitreous, vitrectomy is typically indicated as vitreous traction poses a risk of retinal detachment.3The patient underwent paracentesis and began treatment with atropine and systemic acetazolamide. One day later, laser iridoplasty was performed in the right eye to remove iris tissue from the tube tip. Postlaser IOP was 8 mm Hg OD. The IOP remained stable, and a few weeks later the patient underwent Descemet stripping endothelial keratoplasty of the right eye with trimming of the tube at the sulcus. Four months after transplantation, she continues to do well. Her visual acuity has improved to 20/60  2 and her IOP remains stable at 6 mm Hg OD by Goldmann applanation. She continues to receive topical atropine.
